ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for commercial cracker products; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of Ritz Crackers is approximately 16 g (about 5 crackers), as indicated on standard product packaging.

nutrient_source.title: Crackers, standard snack-type — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 18228)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, dietary fibre, sodium) are taken directly from the Singapore HPB Foodo database entry for Ritz biscuit (any flavour), which is the highest-priority approved source for this Singapore-catalogued product. Saturated fat is cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for standard snack-type crackers (NDB 18228) and USDA Branded Food data for Ritz Original Crackers. Calcium and iron are reported as 0.0 mg per the HPB source. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, potassium, and micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1 (data not available in the primary source).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 70 for Ritz-type crackers is consistent with published values for plain wheat crackers in the University of Sydney GI Database and the 2008 International GI Tables (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (70 × 9.21) / 100 ≈ 6.5, based on a 16 g serving containing approximately 9.21 g available carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium content at 1015 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Ritz Crackers contain 1015 mg sodium per 100 g — well above the 600 mg/100 g threshold for hypertension concern — meaning a single 16 g serving delivers approximately 162 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should avoid regular consumption and choose low-sodium crackers (≤120 mg/100 g) instead.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load (57.6 g/100 g), zero dietary fibre, and high GI (~70)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ritz Crackers are made from refined wheat flour with zero dietary fibre and a high glycaemic index of approximately 70, producing rapid postprandial glucose spikes; people with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid them as a regular snack and substitute whole-grain crackers with dietary fibre ≥3 g/100 g and a lower GI.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~9.5 g/100 g from palm oil), high sodium (1015 mg/100 g), and ultra-processed formulation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of high saturated fat from palm oil (~9.5 g/100 g), very high sodium (1015 mg/100 g), and ultra-processed ingredients makes Ritz Crackers unfavourable for cardiovascular health; individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit intake to occasional small portions and prioritise whole-grain, low-sodium alternatives.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat, high sodium, zero fibre, and potential trans fat in some regional formulations
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ritz Crackers contain high saturated fat (~9.5 g/100 g) and very high sodium (1015 mg/100 g), both of which are pro-inflammatory; some regional formulations may contain partially hydrogenated oils contributing trans fat, which is a hard disqualifier for rheumatoid arthritis management, and patients should avoid this product entirely.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ritz Crackers are thin, round, lightly salted baked wheat crackers produced by Nabisco (Mondelez International), originally launched in the United States in 1934 and now sold globally including in Singapore. They are made primarily from enriched wheat flour, vegetable oil (predominantly palm oil), sugar, salt, and leavening agents, giving them a characteristic buttery, slightly sweet, and salty flavour with a crisp, flaky texture. The crackers are available in multiple flavour variants including original, cheese, whole wheat, and various regional editions. From a nutritional standpoint, Ritz Crackers are energy-dense at approximately 508 kcal per 100 g, with a high carbohydrate content of 57.6 g/100 g derived almost entirely from refined wheat flour, zero dietary fibre, 10.3 g sugar/100 g, and a high fat content of 25.7 g/100 g of which approximately 9.5 g is saturated fat from palm oil. The sodium content is extremely high at 1015 mg/100 g, making them clinically significant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycaemic index is approximately 70 (high), producing a meaningful glycaemic load even in small servings. Ritz Crackers are classified as ultra-processed food and are not suitable for regular consumption by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. They are commonly consumed as a standalone snack, paired with cheese, dips, or spreads, and are a popular party and lunchbox item across many cultures.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in Ritz crackers?
Are Ritz crackers high in fat?
Is Ritz cracker suitable for hypertension?
How many calories in 5 Ritz crackers?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from the Singapore HPB Foodo database, which is the highest-priority approved source for Singapore-catalogued food products. Saturated fat cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for standard snack crackers. GI value of 70 is consistent with published University of Sydney GI Database values for wheat crackers. Confidence is high for all primary macro fields.
notes: dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — Ritz Crackers are made from refined wheat flour with no added fibre, confirmed zero per Singapore HPB Foodo database. calcium_mg and iron_mg are reported as 0.0 per HPB source; these may reflect rounding to zero in the HPB database rather than absolute absence, but are retained as reported. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, and all vitamin and mineral micronutrients not listed above are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, cholesterol, potassium, and micronutrient data for this specific HPB entry are not available in the Singapore HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods as of 2026-04-16. saturated_fat_g of 9.5 g/100 g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for standard snack-type crackers and USDA Branded Food data for Ritz Original Crackers, as HPB does not report saturated fat separately. Trans fat presence is plausible in some regional Ritz formulations using partially hydrogenated oils; consumers should verify current product label. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in HPB source.
